title: Iran Says 'We Are One Soul' After Trump Claims Leaders Are Fighting Each Other
summary:
Have you ever had someone say you and your best friend are fighting — when you're actually totally fine? That's what's happening between the U.S. and Iran right now.

President Trump has been saying that Iran's leaders are arguing with each other. He called it "crazy infighting" between "moderates" and "hardliners." He even claimed Iranians are "having a very hard time figuring out who their leader is."

But Iran's top officials fired back — and they did it in a really coordinated way. Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, and the Supreme National Security Council all posted the exact same message on X (formerly Twitter). It said: "In Iran, there are no radicals or moderates. We are all 'Iranian' and 'revolutionary.'"

Iran's first vice president added in English: "We are one soul, one nation."

Why does this matter? Because if Iran's leaders are united, it makes it harder for Trump to say they're too weak to negotiate. Iran also says talks aren't happening because the U.S. is blockading its ports (blocking ships from coming in or out).

Meanwhile, Trump said the U.S. military will "shoot and kill" anyone laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz — a narrow waterway where a lot of oil ships pass. That's a big threat, and it could make things even more tense.

So who's right? Is Iran united or divided? It's hard to know for sure, but one thing is clear: both sides are talking tough.
why_it_matters: This shows how countries use social media to argue and shape what people believe, which can affect whether there is war or peace.

keywords.explanation: a country in the Middle East whose leaders say they are united
the U.S. president who claimed Iran's leaders are fighting
the top officials of a country, like Iran's president and parliament speaker
people who want smaller changes and less conflict
people who want strong, strict actions and refuse to compromise

How did Iran's leaders respond to Trump's claim?
options: They all posted the same message on social media

background_read: The United States and Iran have been rivals for many years, often arguing over Iran's nuclear program and its support for groups like Hezbollah.
The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway where many oil tankers pass. Blocking it could cause oil prices to rise around the world.
A blockade is when one country stops ships from entering or leaving another country's ports.
Article_Structure: WHO: Iran's leaders (President, Parliament Speaker, National Security Council) and President Trump
WHAT: Iran's leaders posted a unified message saying they are not divided, after Trump claimed infighting
WHERE: On social media platform X (formerly Twitter)
WHY: To show that Iran is united and to challenge Trump's narrative that they are weak

perspectives.description: Iran wants the world to see its leaders as strong and united, not weak and divided.
Trump says Iran is divided, which might be a way to pressure them or justify tough actions.
It's hard to know who is telling the truth, but both sides are talking tough and the situation is tense.
